talk_title: Serial Plasma Comprehensive Genomic Profiling Detects Therapy Resistance and Guides Management of Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer

speaker.name: William Bowers
speaker.affiliation: Johns Hopkins University, Sidney Kimmel Comprehensive Cancer Center

biomarkers.name: ctDNA detectability (mutation count)
biomarkers.finding: Undetectable ctDNA associated with significantly better overall survival

biomarkers.name: Actionable alteration on plasma
biomarkers.finding: Patients with actionable alteration who received genotype‑matched therapy had significantly improved overall survival

clinical_data.metric: unique clinical utility of plasma genotyping

MOA_summary: Serial plasma comprehensive genomic profiling (PCGP) using Guardant 360 detects actionable and resistance alterations (e.g., EGFR, MET, EGFR T790M, C797S, BRAF) in metastatic NSCLC, enabling genotype‑matched therapy selection and demonstrating that ctDNA detectability correlates with overall survival.
key_findings: 37% of NSCLC patients had an actionable alteration on first plasma PCGP.
In treatment‑naive patients, 36% harbored FDA‑approved genotype‑matched alterations.
22% of treatment‑naive patients started genotype‑matched therapy within 30 days of plasma result.
Undetectable ctDNA was associated with significantly longer overall survival versus detectable ctDNA.
Patients receiving genotype‑matched therapy based on plasma findings had significantly improved overall survival.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: MET amplification and secondary EGFR alterations were the most common resistance mechanisms to osimertinib.
Resistance landscapes differed between first/second‑generation EGFR TKIs (T790M‑driven) and osimertinib (MET, C797S).
open_questions: How does plasma PCGP performance compare with tissue NGS in low‑tumor‑burden settings?
Can earlier serial sampling improve detection of emerging resistance?

talk_title: Feasibility of Plasma ctDNA for Detecting Brain Metastasis Specific Alterations in Lung Cancer

speaker.name: Dr. Stabile
speaker.affiliation: University of Pittsburgh Hillman Cancer Center

biomarkers.name: MET amplification in plasma ctDNA
biomarkers.finding: Detected more frequently in patients with brain metastases than without

models.name: retrospective metastatic NSCLC ctDNA cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 28 patients with paired pre/post‑craniotomy plasma and brain tissue

clinical_data.metric: MET amplification detection in plasma at progression
clinical_data.population: patients with progression after EGFR TKI

clinical_data.metric: MET amplification in brain metastasis tissue
MET amplification detection in matched plasma (complete sets)
clinical_data.population: patients with paired tissue and plasma

clinical_data.metric: MET amplification detection in all plasma (including partial sets)

MOA_summary: Plasma ctDNA profiling detects MET amplifications, which are enriched in lung cancer brain metastases; radiation may enhance ctDNA shedding, improving detection of brain‑specific alterations.
key_findings: MET amplification is significantly more common in lung cancer brain metastases (16.4%) than in primary tumors (3.7%).
Plasma ctDNA detects MET amplifications in ~10% of patients at progression, but concordance with tissue is limited.
In paired pre‑ and post‑craniotomy samples, only 1 of 2 tissue MET amplifications were captured in plasma.
Radiation therapy appears to increase ctDNA shedding, leading to higher VAF/CNV signals post‑radiation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Technical challenges capture MET amplification by ctDNA, especially with low tumor burden or intrathoracic disease.
open_questions: Can assay sensitivity be improved to reliably detect brain‑specific MET amplifications?
What is the optimal timing of plasma collection relative to radiotherapy for maximal ctDNA yield?

talk_title: AXL mRNA Overexpression in CTCs as a New Potential Liquid Biopsy Biomarker in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer

biomarkers.name: AXL mRNA in CTCs
biomarkers.finding: Detected in ~13% baseline, 16% after first osimertinib cycle, 9% during treatment, 12% at progression

biomarkers.name: AXL mRNA in CTCs during immunotherapy
biomarkers.finding: Low baseline; increased after anti‑PD‑1 in a few patients

models.name: NSCLC patient CTC cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: Group A: osimertinib-treated (multiple time points); Group B: immunotherapy-treated

clinical_data.metric: AXL overexpression after first osimertinib cycle

MOA_summary: AXL mRNA overexpression in size‑enriched circulating tumor cells (CTCs) is detected by RT‑qPCR and rises after osimertinib exposure, suggesting a role in EMT‑mediated resistance and providing a potential biomarker for selecting AXL‑targeted therapies.
readouts: Droplet digital PCR for EGFR mutations
key_findings: AXL mRNA is overexpressed in CTCs of ~13% of NSCLC patients at baseline and increases after the first osimertinib cycle.
AXL overexpression persists in a subset of patients at disease progression, indicating a possible resistance mechanism.
During anti‑PD‑1 therapy, AXL overexpression emerges in a few patients, suggesting adaptive resistance.
CTC‑derived AXL expression provides complementary information to ctDNA and tissue analyses.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: AXL overexpression is linked to EMT and resistance to EGFR TKIs and immune checkpoint inhibitors.

combination_strategies.rationale: Counteract EMT‑driven resistance mediated by AXL

open_questions: What is the therapeutic threshold of AXL mRNA expression in CTCs for initiating AXL‑targeted therapy?
How does CTC‑derived AXL expression correlate with tissue AXL protein levels?

talk_title: Circulating Tumor Cell‑Informed Pipeline for Rapid Personalization of Synergistic Cytokine‑Armed CAR‑NK Cell Therapy in Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma

models.name: BxPC3 (MUC4+), AsPC1 (mesothelin+)
models.genotype_or_key_features: PDAC lines used for killing assays

MOA_summary: CTC enrichment via a microfluidic Labyrinth device identifies mesothelin or MUC4 expression, guiding selection of cytokine‑armed CAR‑NK cells (IL‑12/IL‑15) that synergistically enhance NK activation, granzyme B release, and long‑term tumor killing in PDAC models.
readouts: Granzyme B and IFN‑γ flow cytometry
key_findings: 87% of PDAC patients had CTCs expressing at least one CAR‑NK target (mesothelin or MUC4).
MUC4‑positive CTCs were enriched in metastatic patients.
IL‑12 and IL‑15 armed CAR‑NK cells achieved near‑complete tumor eradication in 2D killing assays and eliminated spheroids by day 12.
Synergistic cytokine‑armed CAR‑NK cells provided superior tumor suppression and survival benefit in mouse models compared with conventional CAR‑NK.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Target expression heterogeneity (exclusive mesothelin vs. MUC4) underscores need for personalized CAR‑NK selection.

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhance NK activation and proliferation for synergistic anti‑tumor effect
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased granzyme B, IFN‑γ, and sustained killing in vitro and in vivo

open_questions: How will CTC target expression evolve during therapy and affect CAR‑NK efficacy?
Can this pipeline be adapted for other solid tumors with distinct CTC antigens?

talk_title: ctDNA Clearance Dynamics in MSI‑High Colorectal Cancer Treated with Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors

biomarkers.name: ctDNA (Signatera tumor‑informed assay)
biomarkers.finding: Early ctDNA negativity after ICI start associated with markedly improved OS (HR 4.7, 4‑yr OS 95% vs 81%)

biomarkers.name: ctDNA clearance (any time)
biomarkers.finding: Patients who cleared ctDNA at any point had 10‑fold OS advantage (HR 10, 4‑yr OS 95.5% vs 66.1%)

clinical_data.metric: OS hazard ratio for early ctDNA negative vs positive
OS hazard ratio for any ctDNA clearance vs persistent positivity

MOA_summary: Longitudinal tumor‑informed ctDNA monitoring (Signatera) in MSI‑high metastatic colorectal cancer reveals that early and sustained ctDNA clearance after immune checkpoint inhibitor initiation predicts markedly improved overall survival.
key_findings: 73% of MSI‑high mCRC patients were ctDNA positive before ICI therapy.
77% achieved ctDNA clearance at least once after ICI start, median time 96 days.
Early clearance (<4 months) occurred in 44% and correlated with 4‑year OS of 95% vs 81% for ctDNA‑positive patients.
Any clearance conferred a 10‑fold OS advantage (95.5% vs 66.1% 4‑yr OS).
Sustained clearance (≥6 months) was observed in 63% of patients.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Persistent ctDNA positivity identified a high‑risk group with markedly inferior survival.
open_questions: Do ctDNA dynamics differ between pembrolizumab monotherapy and ipilimumab/nivolumab combination?
Can ctDNA monitoring guide optimal duration of ICI therapy in MSI‑high mCRC?

talk_title: Genomic Signatures in Red Blood Cell DNA‑Enabled Early Non‑Invasive Detection of Colorectal Neoplasia

biomarkers.name: RBC‑DNA genomic signature
biomarkers.assay: Low‑coverage whole‑genome sequencing of RBC DNA
biomarkers.finding: AUC 0.95 for advanced colorectal neoplasia; 85% sensitivity for advanced adenomas, 95% for CRC at 90% specificity

clinical_data.metric: AUC for advanced colorectal neoplasia (internal test)
Advanced adenoma sensitivity in external cohorts

clinical_data.value: Higher sensitivity for advanced adenomas and Stage 1 CRC

MOA_summary: Red blood cell‑associated DNA fragments, released during erythropoiesis under tumor‑induced genotoxic stress, carry distinct genomic coverage patterns that differ between colorectal cancer patients and healthy controls, enabling a sequencing‑based classifier to detect advanced neoplasia with high accuracy.
readouts: Low‑coverage whole‑genome sequencing of RBC DNA
key_findings: RBC DNA fragments are shorter (avg 4.3 kb) than genomic DNA but longer than cfDNA.
Distinct genome‑wide coverage patterns differentiate CRC patients from healthy donors.
A binary classifier achieved AUC 0.95, 85% sensitivity for advanced adenomas and 95% for CRC at 90% specificity.
Performance remained robust across external validation cohorts and was not confounded by blood counts or lifestyle factors.
In a prospective head‑to‑head comparison, RBC‑DNA testing outperformed FIT for detecting advanced adenomas and Stage 1 CRC.
open_questions: What are the specific genomic regions driving the RBC‑DNA signal and their biological relevance?
Can RBC‑DNA signatures be extended to other cancer types or pan‑cancer early detection?
